runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 8 in a U.S. microsimulation model.
citations: My prior calibration intuition from labor supply and substitution elasticities used in tax-benefit microsimulation
General empirical patterns that substitution responses are moderate for mid-to-upper income deciles under phase-outs and marginal tax rate variation
Mechanical sensitivity of simulation responses to effective marginal wage changes
runs.reasoningSummary: I put the median slightly below 1 with a moderate right tail: decile 8 primary earners typically face nontrivial but not extreme marginal-rate variation, yielding elasticity-like response coefficients commonly in the ~0.5 to ~1.5 range.

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 8 in a U.S. microsimulation model, i.e., the response coefficient multiplying the bounded relative change in the effective marginal wage for primary earners in decile 8 under a current-law U.S. tax-benefit simulation (elasticity units).
citations: Recall anchor: typical labor-supply/earnings response elasticities for prime-age workers are often modest in magnitude
Recall anchor: microsimulation substitution responses commonly vary by income decile with higher elasticities for upper-middle deciles
Recall anchor: bounded marginal-wage change elasticities often imply sub-unitary substitution-response coefficients (less than 1 in many calibrations)
runs.reasoningSummary: I assume a modest, sub-unity elasticity for decile 8 because primary-earner labor supply and participation typically respond to marginal incentives but not as strongly as more discretionary margins; the upper tail allows for some calibrations that imply stronger substitution effects.

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 8 in a U.S. microsimulation model.
citations: AET (Agregate income tax) micro and labor-supply elasticities intuition (substitution vs. income effects)
Empirical labor supply substitution elasticities for working-age primary earners (order-of-magnitude memory)
Typical calibration ranges for tax-benefit microsimulations mapping EMW changes to labor supply responses
runs.reasoningSummary: I place the decile-8 primary-earner elasticity slightly below 1 on median, with moderate spread: limited by less responsiveness than extreme lower-income earners and not fully rigid among upper-middle earners; the upper tail allows for stronger substitution responses under larger EMW shifts.
